topic: Unit 4: Internal course - Comparative and Contextual Study
subject: english-literature

items.q: Why does historical context matter when reading literature?
items.correct: Texts emerge from and respond to specific historical conditions — context illuminates references, assumptions and arguments that might otherwise be invisible.
items.misconception: Context just means writing about what the author had for breakfast.
items.random_wrong: Context is required because all texts are about history.
items.why: Students treat context as biographical trivia. Useful context is what the text engages with — politics, ideology, genre conventions — not personal detail of the author's life.

items.correct: When a sentence or phrase runs over from one line of poetry to the next without a punctuated pause, creating flow or surprise across the line break.
items.misconception: Enjambment is when a poem has very long lines.
items.random_wrong: Enjambment is when a poem uses no punctuation at all.
items.why: Enjambment is about syntax crossing the line break, not line length or punctuation in general. Students miss that it is about the relationship between grammar and lineation.

items.q: What is the difference between a theme and a symbol?
items.correct: A theme is an abstract idea or concern the text explores (e.g. mortality); a symbol is a concrete image that stands for something else (e.g. a skull).
items.misconception: Themes are just symbols repeated through the book.
items.random_wrong: A theme is the title of the book.
items.why: Students treat theme and symbol as the same thing. Themes are ideas, symbols are images. Skulls (symbol) can develop the theme (mortality), but they are not the same level of abstraction.
The answer examiners want is: Greenblatt. “Tillyard” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the British Marxist Shakespeare school?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cultural materialism. “New historicism” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Political Shakespeare published?
items.why: The real figure is 1985 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
